summary: Drew DeVault's August 2022 status update is a brief check-in during a quieter month due to moving house. The main technical highlights are Thread-Local Storage (TLS) implementation for qbe (Hare's compiler backend) and significant progress on Helios, his microkernel OS project. He also mentions starting a GameBoy emulator in Hare as a fun side project. Hare development has slowed due to a backlog of 123 emails in the patch queue.
key_points: Implemented Thread-Local Storage (TLS) for qbe, Hare's compiler backend, primarily needed for Helios's driver library threading support
Helios microkernel received TLS runtime support, capability copying/moving, and progress toward capability destructors with page reclamation
Started a GameBoy emulator written in Hare as a recreational side project
Hare patch queue is backed up at 123 emails, slowing standard library progress
money_quotes: It is a blessedly cool morning here in Amsterdam.
No promises on when it will be functional or how much I plan on working on it – just doing it for fun.
Hare's standard library does not support multi-threading, but I needed this for Helios, whose driver library does support threads.
Next goal is to finish up all of this capability work so that you can freely create, copy, move, and destroy capabilities, then use all of these features to implement a simple shell.
I'm currently looking at a patch queue 123 emails backed up.
key_insight: Even in a quiet month, DeVault's work spans multiple layers of the systems stack — from compiler backends to microkernel capability management — illustrating the breadth of his from-scratch systems programming ambitions.
